You are a developer exploring `jj`'s ability to handle multi-parent merges (octopus merges). A repository already exists at `/home/user/octopus_repo` with a base commit containing a file `base.txt`. Your task is to create 3 concurrent bookmarks named `branch_a`, `branch_b`, and `branch_c` from the base commit. In each bookmark, add a new file (`a.txt`, `b.txt`, `c.txt` respectively) with some content. Finally, create a new commit that has all 3 bookmarks as parents, and bookmark it as `octopus_merge`. Verify the merge by checking the parents of `octopus_merge` and ensuring all files are present in the working copy.

The user wants me to explore `jj`'s ability to handle multi-parent merges (octopus merges). Let me break down the task:

1. Work with a repository at `/home/user/octopus_repo`
2. Create 3 concurrent bookmarks (`branch_a`, `branch_b`, `branch_c`) from the base commit
3. In each bookmark, add a new file (`a.txt`, `b.txt`, `c.txt`)
4. Create a new commit with all 3 bookmarks as parents (octopus merge)
5. Bookmark the merge as `octopus_merge`
6. Verify by checking parents and ensuring all files are present

First, I should check if the repository exists and understand its current state.
I'll help you explore `jj`'s octopus merge capabilities. Let me start by checking the repository state and then set up the scenario.

todos.content: Check repository state at /home/user/octopus_repo
Create 3 bookmarks (branch_a, branch_b, branch_c) from base commit
Add a.txt, b.txt, c.txt in respective bookmarks
Create octopus merge with all 3 bookmarks as parents
Bookmark the merge as octopus_merge
Verify merge by checking parents and files

output.output: Command exited with code 2

parts.text: NoneHmm, let me try a different approach. I need to understand how `jj` bookmarks work better. Let me check the jj documentation or help.

output.output:
Move existing bookmarks to target revision

Unlike `jj bookmark set`, this command cannot create new bookmarks.

If bookmark names are given, the specified bookmarks will be updated to point to the target
revision.

If `--from` options are given, bookmarks currently pointing to the specified revisions will be
updated. The bookmarks can also be filtered by names.

Example: pull up the nearest bookmarks to the working-copy parent

$ jj bookmark move --from 'heads(::@- & bookmarks())' --to @-

Usage: jj bookmark move [OPTIONS] <NAMES|--from <REVSETS>>

Arguments:
  [NAMES]...
          Move bookmarks matching the given name patterns
          
          By default, the specified pattern matches bookmark names with glob syntax. You can also
          use other [string pattern syntax].
          
          [string pattern syntax]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/revsets/#string-patterns

Options:
  -f, --from <REVSETS>
          Move bookmarks from the given revisions

  -t, --to <REVSET>
          Move bookmarks to this revision
          
          [default: @]

  -B, --allow-backwards
          Allow moving bookmarks backwards or sideways

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

```
jj bookmark move branch_a --to @
```

Let me delete these bookmarks first and recreate them at the right commit. The issue is that I created them at the root commit `@-` not at `ynwutkuy`. Let me delete them first.

parts.text:
NoneI see that a new commit was created, but my changes to a.txt are on the parent (ynwutkuy). The issue is that `jj new` creates a new commit on top of the target, but it doesn't carry over the working copy changes. In `jj`, I need to first describe/commit the changes on branch_a, then create new commits on top of the base for the other branches.

Let me rethink this: Since we have diverged history with each branch, I need to go back to the base commit and create each branch independently. Let me try a different approach.

In jj, I'll use `jj new` to create commits on top of the base commit, each with their respective files.

parts.text:
None... Hmm, it seems like the `jj new` command is creating a new commit. Let me rethink my approach.

Actually, let me understand jj better. When I do `jj new branch_b`, it creates a new commit *after* branch_b as a parent. But what I want is to have concurrent bookmarks all pointing to the same base commit.

Let me check:
1. I want branch_a, branch_b, branch_c all pointing to the base commit 6aeca5d9
2. Then configure each with different files
3. Then merge all 3

It seems like I already have this setup - all 3 bookmarks point to the same commit (6aeca5d9). But the issue is I committed a.txt to the current working copy, so branch_a now includes a.txt.

Let me think about the jj model better. In jj, bookmarks point to commits. Each commit has changes (like diffs). If I edit a commit at branch_a, branch_b, or branch_c, all of them move together because they point to the same commit.

Let me take a different approach. I'll create separate commits for each bookmark, where each commit is based off the base commit with a different file added.

Hmm, I need to reconsider my approach. Looking at the `jj status` output again:
- Working copy changes show A (Added) status for a.txt and base.txt
- This means these changes exist in jj's view but haven't been committed

The working copy (`@`) represents the current commit being edited. When I add a.txt, it's added to the working copy commit. But I need each bookmark to point to a commit with its specific file.

Let me think about this more carefully. In jj:
- `@` is the working copy commit
- For branch_a, I need a commit with a.txt
- For branch_b, I need a commit with b.txt
- For branch_c, I need a commit with c.txt

Currently all bookmarks point to the same commit (ynwutkuy) which has both a.txt and base.txt.

I think the issue is that I need to create separate commits for each bookmark. Let me try a different approach:
1. Get the base commit ID
2. Create new commits from the base for each bookmark
3. Add the respective files to each

None I need to rethink this. The issue is that jj creates a new commit for each bookmark, but that new commit is not actually the bookmark commit - it creates a child commit. In jj, bookmarks point to commits, not working copies. 

The workflow should be:
1. Create three separate child commits from the base, each with a bookmark
2. Each commit will have its own file

Let me approach this differently - I'll create the three branches as commits using `jj new` with the bookmark names.

NoneGood, now the repository is properly set up. The base commit is `lnuztosp` with the file `base.txt`. Now I need to:
1. Create 3 bookmarks (branch_a, branch_b, branch_c) from the base commit
2. Add the corresponding files to each bookmark
3. Create an octopus merge

Let me set up the bookmarks now.

todos.content: Check repository state at /home/user/octopus_repo
Create 3 bookmarks (branch_a, branch_b, branch_c) from base commit
Add a.txt, b.txt, c.txt in respective bookmarks
Create octopus merge with all 3 bookmarks as parents
Bookmark the merge as octopus_merge
Verify merge by checking parents and files

input.command: jj bookmark create branch_a -r @- && jj bookmark create branch_b -r @- && jj bookmark create branch_c -r @-

Good. Let me check the parent of `@` and decide the approach. The current working copy is at the child of `master`. I need to create commits on each branch from `master`. Let me do:

1. First, just work with `@` which is on top of master - let me `jj new` to get back to master for branch_a:

input.command: jj new master --config user.email="test@example.com" --config user.name="Test User" && echo "Content from branch A" > a.txt && jj st
